kids.question: Who wrote the letter to the Galatians?
kids.explanation: Galatians 1:1 — "Paul, an apostle, (not of men, neither by man, but by Jesus Christ)."

kids.question: What did Paul say he was amazed about?
kids.option_a: That the Galatians were turning to a different gospel
kids.option_b: That they had built a new church
kids.option_c: That they were growing so fast
kids.option_d: That they had sent him a gift
kids.explanation: Galatians 1:6 — "I marvel that ye are so soon removed from him that called you into the grace of Christ unto another gospel."

kids.question: Who did Paul say taught him the gospel?
kids.explanation: Galatians 1:12 — "I neither received it of man, neither was I taught it, but by the revelation of Jesus Christ."

teens.question: What did Paul say should happen to anyone who preaches a different gospel?
teens.option_a: Let him be accursed (anathema)
teens.explanation: Galatians 1:8 — "though we, or an angel from heaven, preach any other gospel… let him be accursed."

teens.question: What was Paul's former life in Judaism characterized by?
teens.option_a: Persecuting the church of God and wasting it
teens.explanation: Galatians 1:13 — "I persecuted the church of God, and wasted it."

teens.question: When did God set Paul apart according to this chapter?
teens.option_a: From his mother's womb
teens.option_b: From the day of his baptism
teens.option_c: From the day he met Jesus on the road
teens.option_d: From the day he entered the synagogue
teens.explanation: Galatians 1:15 — "God, who separated me from my mother's womb, and called me by his grace."

teens.question: Where did Paul go after his conversion instead of going to Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: Arabia, and then back to Damascus
teens.explanation: Galatians 1:17 — "I went into Arabia, and returned again unto Damascus."

teens.question: Which apostle did Paul visit when he finally went to Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: Peter (Cephas), and he also saw James the Lord's brother
teens.explanation: Galatians 1:18-19 — "I went up to Jerusalem to see Peter… But other of the apostles saw I none, save James the Lord's brother."

adults.question: How does Paul establish his apostolic authority in the opening verse?
adults.option_a: By stating his apostleship is not from men nor through man, but through Jesus Christ and God the Father who raised Him
adults.option_b: By listing the miracles he performed
adults.option_c: By citing his training under Gamaliel
adults.option_d: By referencing his letter of recommendation from Jerusalem
adults.explanation: Galatians 1:1 — "Paul, an apostle, (not of men, neither by man, but by Jesus Christ, and God the Father, who raised him from the dead)."

adults.question: What does Paul say Christ gave Himself for?
adults.option_a: To deliver us from this present evil world, according to the will of God and our Father
adults.option_b: To establish an earthly kingdom in Israel
adults.option_c: To abolish all forms of government
adults.option_d: To give mankind wealth and prosperity
adults.explanation: Galatians 1:4 — "Who gave himself for our sins, that he might deliver us from this present evil world, according to the will of God and our Father."

adults.question: How quickly does Paul say the Galatians were removed from the gospel of grace?
adults.option_a: "So soon" — emphasizing the rapid nature of their departure
adults.option_b: After many years of faithful service
adults.option_c: Gradually over a generation
adults.option_d: Only after Paul left the region
adults.explanation: Galatians 1:6 — "I marvel that ye are so soon removed from him that called you into the grace of Christ unto another gospel."

adults.question: What rhetorical question does Paul ask about seeking to please men?
adults.option_a: "If I yet pleased men, I should not be the servant of Christ"
adults.option_b: "Can a man serve two masters at once?"
adults.option_c: "What profit is there in gaining the whole world?"
adults.option_d: "Who among you is without sin?"
adults.explanation: Galatians 1:10 — "for if I yet pleased men, I should not be the servant of Christ."

adults.question: How does Paul describe his zeal in Judaism compared to his contemporaries?
adults.option_a: He profited in the Jews' religion above many his equals, being exceedingly zealous of the traditions of his fathers
adults.option_b: He was the youngest rabbi ever ordained
adults.option_c: He had memorized the entire Torah by age twelve
adults.option_d: He was recognized as the chief Pharisee by the Sanhedrin
adults.explanation: Galatians 1:14 — "profited in the Jews' religion above many my equals in mine own nation, being more exceedingly zealous of the traditions of my fathers."

adults.question: What was God's purpose in revealing His Son in Paul?
adults.option_a: That Paul might preach Him among the heathen (Gentiles)
adults.option_b: That Paul might reform the Jewish temple system
adults.option_c: That Paul might write the entire New Testament
adults.option_d: That Paul might become the high priest of Israel
adults.explanation: Galatians 1:16 — "To reveal his Son in me, that I might preach him among the heathen."

adults.question: How long was it before Paul went up to Jerusalem after his conversion?
adults.option_a: Three years
adults.option_b: Forty days
adults.option_c: Seven years
adults.option_d: One year
adults.explanation: Galatians 1:18 — "Then after three years I went up to Jerusalem to see Peter."

adults.question: What was the reaction of the churches in Judaea regarding Paul?
adults.option_a: They glorified God in him, having heard that their former persecutor now preached the faith he once destroyed
adults.option_b: They were suspicious and refused to meet him
adults.option_c: They sent a delegation to test his theology
adults.option_d: They immediately appointed him as their bishop
adults.explanation: Galatians 1:23-24 — "He which persecuted us in times past now preacheth the faith which once he destroyed. And they glorified God in me."

kids.question: Who did Paul take with him to Jerusalem?
kids.explanation: Galatians 2:1 — "I went up again to Jerusalem with Barnabas, and took Titus with me also."

kids.question: What did Paul say we are not saved by?
kids.explanation: Galatians 2:16 — "a man is not justified by the works of the law, but by the faith of Jesus Christ."

kids.question: Who does Paul say now lives in him?
kids.explanation: Galatians 2:20 — "I am crucified with Christ: nevertheless I live; yet not I, but Christ liveth in me."

teens.question: Why did Paul rebuke Peter (Cephas) to his face in Antioch?
teens.option_a: Because Peter withdrew from eating with Gentiles when Jewish believers arrived from James
teens.option_c: Because Peter refused to preach to the Gentiles
teens.option_d: Because Peter was teaching false doctrine
teens.explanation: Galatians 2:11-12 — "I withstood him to the face, because he was to be blamed. For before that certain came from James, he did eat with the Gentiles: but when they were come, he withdrew."

teens.question: What three pillars of the church gave Paul the right hand of fellowship?
teens.option_a: James, Cephas (Peter), and John
teens.explanation: Galatians 2:9 — "James, Cephas, and John, who seemed to be pillars… gave to me and Barnabas the right hands of fellowship."

teens.question: Was Titus compelled to be circumcised even though he was a Greek?
teens.option_a: No, he was not compelled
teens.explanation: Galatians 2:3 — "But neither Titus, who was with me, being a Greek, was compelled to be circumcised."

teens.question: What did the Jerusalem apostles ask Paul to remember?
teens.option_a: The poor, which Paul was also eager to do
teens.explanation: Galatians 2:10 — "Only they would that we should remember the poor; the same which I also was forward to do."

teens.question: According to Galatians 2:20, how does Paul now live in the flesh?
teens.option_a: By the faith of the Son of God, who loved him and gave Himself for him
teens.option_b: By keeping the law of Moses perfectly
teens.option_c: By following the traditions of the elders
teens.explanation: Galatians 2:20 — "the life which I now live in the flesh I live by the faith of the Son of God, who loved me, and gave himself for me."

adults.question: How many years after his first visit did Paul go up to Jerusalem again?
adults.option_a: Fourteen years
adults.option_b: Seven years
adults.option_c: Three years
adults.option_d: Twenty years
adults.explanation: Galatians 2:1 — "Then fourteen years after I went up again to Jerusalem."

adults.question: Why did Paul privately communicate the gospel he preached among the Gentiles to the leaders?
adults.option_a: Lest by any means he should run, or had run, in vain
adults.option_b: Because he doubted his own message
adults.option_c: Because the leaders demanded an accounting
adults.option_d: Because he needed their financial support
adults.explanation: Galatians 2:2 — "lest by any means I should run, or had run, in vain."

adults.question: Who does Paul describe as "false brethren unawares brought in"?
adults.option_a: Those who came in privately to spy out the liberty believers had in Christ Jesus, to bring them into bondage
adults.option_b: Roman spies infiltrating the church
adults.option_c: Former pagans who had not truly converted
adults.option_d: Tax collectors working for Rome
adults.explanation: Galatians 2:4 — "false brethren unawares brought in, who came in privily to spy out our liberty which we have in Christ Jesus, that they might bring us into bondage."

adults.question: What does Paul say about those who "seemed to be somewhat" (the pillars)?
adults.option_a: Whatever they were, it makes no matter — God accepteth no man's person
adults.option_b: They were the highest authority and must be obeyed
adults.option_c: They were less knowledgeable than Paul
adults.option_d: They had lost their apostolic authority
adults.explanation: Galatians 2:6 — "But of these who seemed to be somewhat, (whatsoever they were, it maketh no matter to me: God accepteth no man's person)."

adults.question: How does Paul distinguish his ministry from Peter's?
adults.option_a: The gospel of the uncircumcision was committed to Paul, as the gospel of the circumcision was to Peter
adults.option_b: Paul was for the educated and Peter for the uneducated
adults.option_c: Paul worked in cities while Peter worked in rural areas
adults.option_d: Paul preached grace while Peter preached law
adults.explanation: Galatians 2:7 — "the gospel of the uncircumcision was committed unto me, as the gospel of the circumcision was unto Peter."

adults.question: What does Paul say Peter's dissimulation led even Barnabas to do?
adults.option_a: Barnabas was also carried away with their dissimulation (hypocrisy)
adults.option_b: Barnabas left the church entirely
adults.option_c: Barnabas confronted the Jerusalem council
adults.option_d: Barnabas returned to Cyprus permanently
adults.explanation: Galatians 2:13 — "And the other Jews dissembled likewise with him; insomuch that Barnabas also was carried away with their dissimulation."

adults.question: What logical argument does Paul make in 2:17-18 about being found sinners?
adults.option_a: If seeking justification by Christ makes us sinners, that does not make Christ a minister of sin — for rebuilding what was destroyed makes one a transgressor
adults.option_b: If we obey the law we cannot sin
adults.option_c: If Christ died for all then all are automatically saved
adults.option_d: If the Gentiles keep the law they become Jews
adults.explanation: Galatians 2:17-18 — "is therefore Christ the minister of sin? God forbid. For if I build again the things which I destroyed, I make myself a transgressor."

adults.question: What does Paul say about the relationship between righteousness and the law?
adults.option_a: "If righteousness come by the law, then Christ is dead in vain"
adults.option_b: "The law and grace work together for righteousness"
adults.option_c: "Righteousness by law was once valid but is now expired"
adults.option_d: "The law prepares us to receive righteousness by works"
adults.explanation: Galatians 2:21 — "if righteousness come by the law, then Christ is dead in vain."

kids.question: Who does Paul say was a man of faith in the Old Testament?
kids.explanation: Galatians 3:6 — "Even as Abraham believed God, and it was accounted to him for righteousness."

kids.question: Are we all one in Christ Jesus?
kids.option_a: Yes — there is neither Jew nor Greek, bond nor free, male nor female
kids.option_c: Only if we follow the law
kids.option_d: Only in heaven, not on earth
kids.explanation: Galatians 3:28 — "There is neither Jew nor Greek… for ye are all one in Christ Jesus."

kids.question: What did Paul call the Galatians for turning away from faith?
kids.explanation: Galatians 3:1 — "O foolish Galatians, who hath bewitched you."

teens.question: How did Paul say the Galatians received the Spirit?
teens.option_a: By the hearing of faith, not by the works of the law
teens.explanation: Galatians 3:2 — "Received ye the Spirit by the works of the law, or by the hearing of faith?"

teens.question: What does Paul say the law was until Christ came?
teens.option_a: A schoolmaster to bring us unto Christ
teens.option_b: A permanent guide for all believers
teens.explanation: Galatians 3:24 — "the law was our schoolmaster to bring us unto Christ, that we might be justified by faith."

teens.question: How many years before the law was the promise given to Abraham?
teens.option_a: Four hundred and thirty years
teens.explanation: Galatians 3:17 — "the covenant, that was confirmed before of God in Christ, the law, which was four hundred and thirty years after, cannot disannul."

teens.question: Who are the children of Abraham according to Paul?
teens.option_a: Those who are of faith
teens.option_b: Only the biological descendants of Abraham
teens.option_c: Only those who keep the law
teens.option_d: Only the twelve tribes of Israel
teens.explanation: Galatians 3:7 — "they which are of faith, the same are the children of Abraham."

teens.question: What curse does Paul say Christ redeemed us from?
teens.option_a: The curse of the law, being made a curse for us
teens.option_d: The curse placed on Adam's ground
teens.explanation: Galatians 3:13 — "Christ hath redeemed us from the curse of the law, being made a curse for us."

adults.question: What Old Testament quote does Paul use to prove that God justifies the Gentiles by faith?
adults.option_a: "In thee shall all nations be blessed" (from Genesis 12:3)
adults.option_b: "The Lord is my shepherd" (Psalm 23:1)
adults.option_c: "The just shall live by faith" (Habakkuk 2:4) — though he quotes this too
adults.option_d: "I will make of thee a great nation" (Genesis 12:2)
adults.explanation: Galatians 3:8 — "the scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the heathen through faith, preached before the gospel unto Abraham, saying, In thee shall all nations be blessed."

adults.question: What Deuteronomy passage does Paul quote about those under the law?
adults.option_a: "Cursed is every one that continueth not in all things which are written in the book of the law to do them" (Deut 27:26)
adults.option_b: "Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God is one Lord" (Deut 6:4)
adults.option_c: "Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thine heart" (Deut 6:5)
adults.option_d: "Man shall not live by bread alone" (Deut 8:3)
adults.explanation: Galatians 3:10 — "Cursed is every one that continueth not in all things which are written in the book of the law to do them."

adults.question: How was Christ's hanging on a tree connected to the law's curse?
adults.option_a: Deuteronomy 21:23 says "Cursed is every one that hangeth on a tree," and Christ bore this curse for us
adults.option_b: Trees were sacred in Jewish law
adults.option_c: The tree symbolized the tree of knowledge in Eden
adults.option_d: Roman law required execution on trees
adults.explanation: Galatians 3:13 — "Christ hath redeemed us from the curse of the law, being made a curse for us: for it is written, Cursed is every one that hangeth on a tree."

adults.question: What distinction does Paul make about God's promise using the word "seed" (singular)?
adults.option_a: The promise was to Abraham's "seed" (singular, meaning Christ), not "seeds" (plural, meaning many)
adults.option_b: The word seed refers to the nation of Israel collectively
adults.option_c: Seed means the church as the new Israel
adults.option_d: Seed refers only to Isaac, Abraham's son
adults.explanation: Galatians 3:16 — "He saith not, And to seeds, as of many; but as of one, And to thy seed, which is Christ."

adults.question: Through whom was the law ordained, according to Paul?
adults.option_a: By angels in the hand of a mediator (Moses)
adults.option_b: Directly by God without any mediator
adults.option_c: Through the prophets over many generations
adults.option_d: Through the high priest Aaron
adults.explanation: Galatians 3:19 — "it was ordained by angels in the hand of a mediator."

adults.question: Is the law against the promises of God, according to Paul?
adults.option_a: God forbid — if a law could have given life, righteousness would have come by the law
adults.option_b: Yes, the law contradicts grace entirely
adults.option_c: Only in certain circumstances
adults.option_d: The law fulfills the promises
adults.explanation: Galatians 3:21 — "Is the law then against the promises of God? God forbid: for if there had been a law given which could have given life, verily righteousness should have been by the law."

adults.question: What does Paul say the Scripture did before faith came?
adults.option_a: It concluded all under sin, that the promise by faith of Jesus Christ might be given to them that believe
adults.option_b: It provided a way of salvation through works
adults.option_c: It divided humanity into chosen and unchosen
adults.option_d: It established the priesthood as mediators
adults.explanation: Galatians 3:22 — "the scripture hath concluded all under sin, that the promise by faith of Jesus Christ might be given to them that believe."

adults.question: What does Paul say about the identity of those who have been baptized into Christ?
adults.option_a: They have put on Christ — neither Jew nor Greek, bond nor free, male nor female, all one in Christ
adults.option_b: They have become spiritual Jews
adults.option_c: They have transcended all earthly relationships
adults.option_d: They no longer have individual identities
adults.explanation: Galatians 3:27-28 — "as many of you as have been baptized into Christ have put on Christ. There is neither Jew nor Greek… for ye are all one in Christ Jesus."

kids.question: Who did God send to redeem those under the law?
kids.option_a: His Son, born of a woman
kids.explanation: Galatians 4:4 — "God sent forth his Son, made of a woman, made under the law."

kids.question: What does Paul say believers can call God?
kids.explanation: Galatians 4:6 — "God hath sent forth the Spirit of his Son into your hearts, crying, Abba, Father."

kids.question: Paul compared two women from the Bible to two covenants. Who were they?
kids.explanation: Galatians 4:22-24 — Abraham had two sons, one by a bondmaid (Hagar) and one by a freewoman (Sarah), which are an allegory of two covenants.

teens.question: What does Paul say believers are no longer, but instead have become?
teens.option_a: No longer servants but sons, and heirs of God through Christ
teens.option_b: No longer sinners but saints by works
teens.option_c: No longer human but spiritual beings
teens.explanation: Galatians 4:7 — "thou art no more a servant, but a son; and if a son, then an heir of God through Christ."

teens.question: What does Paul fear about the Galatians in verse 11?
teens.option_a: That he has laboured upon them in vain
teens.option_c: That they will lose their possessions
teens.explanation: Galatians 4:11 — "I am afraid of you, lest I have bestowed upon you labour in vain."

teens.question: What does Paul say the Galatians would have done for him when he first preached to them?
teens.option_a: Plucked out their own eyes and given them to him
teens.explanation: Galatians 4:15 — "if it had been possible, ye would have plucked out your own eyes, and have given them to me."

teens.question: In Paul's allegory, what does Hagar represent?
teens.option_a: Mount Sinai in Arabia, which genders to bondage (the old covenant of law)
teens.explanation: Galatians 4:24-25 — "the one from the mount Sinai, which gendereth to bondage, which is Agar. For this Agar is mount Sinai in Arabia."

teens.question: What does Paul say about the Jerusalem above?
teens.option_a: She is free and is the mother of us all
teens.option_b: She is bound by the law of Moses
teens.option_c: She is reserved only for Israel
teens.option_d: She has not yet been built
teens.explanation: Galatians 4:26 — "But Jerusalem which is above is free, which is the mother of us all."

adults.question: What does Paul mean by "the fulness of the time" in verse 4?
adults.option_a: The divinely appointed moment when God sent His Son, born of a woman, born under the law, to redeem those under the law
adults.option_b: The end of the world
adults.option_c: The completion of the Old Testament canon
adults.option_d: The moment the Roman Empire reached full power
adults.explanation: Galatians 4:4 — "when the fulness of the time was come, God sent forth his Son, made of a woman, made under the law."

adults.question: What are the "weak and beggarly elements" Paul warns the Galatians about returning to?
adults.option_a: The elementary principles of the world — observing days, months, times, and years as religious obligations
adults.option_b: Pagan idol worship
adults.option_c: Greek philosophy
adults.option_d: Roman government service
adults.explanation: Galatians 4:9-10 — "how turn ye again to the weak and beggarly elements… Ye observe days, and months, and times, and years."

adults.question: What physical infirmity does Paul reference as the occasion for first preaching to the Galatians?
adults.option_a: An infirmity of the flesh, which the Galatians did not despise or reject but received him as an angel of God
adults.option_b: Blindness from his Damascus road experience
adults.option_c: A broken leg from being stoned
adults.option_d: A fever he contracted at sea
adults.explanation: Galatians 4:13-14 — "through infirmity of the flesh I preached the gospel unto you at the first. And my temptation which was in my flesh ye despised not, nor rejected."

adults.question: What does Paul say the false teachers want to do to the Galatians?
adults.option_a: Exclude them so the Galatians would zealously seek the false teachers instead
adults.option_b: Force them to pay temple taxes
adults.option_c: Make them leave their homes
adults.option_d: Turn them over to the Roman authorities
adults.explanation: Galatians 4:17 — "They zealously affect you, but not well; yea, they would exclude you, that ye might affect them."

adults.question: What does Paul compare his anguish for the Galatians to?
adults.option_a: Travailing in birth again until Christ be formed in them
adults.option_b: A shepherd searching for lost sheep
adults.option_c: A farmer waiting for harvest
adults.option_d: A soldier preparing for battle
adults.explanation: Galatians 4:19 — "My little children, of whom I travail in birth again until Christ be formed in you."

adults.question: In the Hagar/Sarah allegory, what does Paul say those born after the flesh did to those born after the Spirit?
adults.option_a: He that was born after the flesh persecuted him that was born after the Spirit
adults.option_b: They lived in peace together
adults.option_c: They divided the inheritance equally
adults.option_d: They both served in the temple
adults.explanation: Galatians 4:29 — "as then he that was born after the flesh persecuted him that was born after the Spirit, even so it is now."

adults.question: What Isaiah prophecy does Paul quote about the barren woman?
adults.option_a: "Rejoice, thou barren that bearest not; break forth and cry, thou that travailest not: for the desolate hath many more children than she which hath an husband" (Isaiah 54:1)
adults.option_b: "A virgin shall conceive and bear a son" (Isaiah 7:14)
adults.option_c: "Unto us a child is born" (Isaiah 9:6)
adults.option_d: "The wilderness shall blossom as the rose" (Isaiah 35:1)
adults.explanation: Galatians 4:27 — "Rejoice, thou barren that bearest not; break forth and cry, thou that travailest not."

adults.question: What does the Scripture say to do with the bondwoman and her son?
adults.option_a: "Cast out the bondwoman and her son: for the son of the bondwoman shall not be heir with the son of the freewoman"
adults.option_b: "Let them dwell together in peace"
adults.option_c: "Give the bondwoman her freedom"
adults.option_d: "Divide the inheritance between them"
adults.explanation: Galatians 4:30 — "Cast out the bondwoman and her son: for the son of the bondwoman shall not be heir with the son of the freewoman."

kids.question: What does Paul say Christ has made us?
kids.explanation: Galatians 5:1 — "Stand fast therefore in the liberty wherewith Christ hath made us free."

kids.question: What is one fruit of the Spirit Paul lists?
kids.explanation: Galatians 5:22 — "the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, longsuffering, gentleness, goodness, faith."

kids.question: What does Paul say the whole law is fulfilled in?
kids.option_a: "Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself"
kids.explanation: Galatians 5:14 — "all the law is fulfilled in one word, even in this; Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself."

teens.question: What does Paul say about those who seek justification by law?
teens.option_a: They are fallen from grace
teens.option_b: They are doing the right thing
teens.explanation: Galatians 5:4 — "whosoever of you are justified by the law; ye are fallen from grace."

teens.question: What does Paul say about circumcision in Christ Jesus?
teens.option_a: Neither circumcision availeth any thing, nor uncircumcision; but faith which worketh by love
teens.option_b: Circumcision is still required for all believers
teens.option_d: Both are equally valid paths to God
teens.explanation: Galatians 5:6 — "in Jesus Christ neither circumcision availeth any thing, nor uncircumcision; but faith which worketh by love."

teens.question: What metaphor does Paul use about false teaching spreading?
teens.option_a: "A little leaven leaveneth the whole lump"
teens.explanation: Galatians 5:9 — "A little leaven leaveneth the whole lump."

teens.question: What are the flesh and the Spirit described as?
teens.option_a: Contrary to one another, so you cannot do the things you would
teens.option_d: Temporary and permanent aspects of life
teens.explanation: Galatians 5:17 — "the flesh lusteth against the Spirit, and the Spirit against the flesh: and these are contrary the one to the other."

teens.question: How many fruits of the Spirit are listed in Galatians 5:22-23?
teens.option_a: Nine: love, joy, peace, longsuffering, gentleness, goodness, faith, meekness, temperance
teens.explanation: Galatians 5:22-23 — "love, joy, peace, longsuffering, gentleness, goodness, faith, meekness, temperance."

adults.question: What does Paul say happens to a person who becomes circumcised for justification?
adults.option_a: Christ shall profit him nothing, and he is a debtor to do the whole law
adults.option_b: He gains extra merit with God
adults.option_c: He fulfills the Abrahamic covenant
adults.option_d: He shows respect for Jewish heritage
adults.explanation: Galatians 5:2-3 — "if ye be circumcised, Christ shall profit you nothing… he is a debtor to do the whole law."

adults.question: What does Paul say believers through the Spirit wait for?
adults.option_a: The hope of righteousness by faith
adults.option_b: The physical return of Christ to Jerusalem
adults.option_c: The restoration of the temple
adults.option_d: The millennial kingdom
adults.explanation: Galatians 5:5 — "we through the Spirit wait for the hope of righteousness by faith."

adults.question: What does Paul say freedom in Christ should not be used as?
adults.option_a: An occasion to the flesh; but by love serve one another
adults.option_b: A reason to stop attending synagogue
adults.option_c: A justification for breaking civil laws
adults.option_d: A basis for spiritual pride
adults.explanation: Galatians 5:13 — "use not liberty for an occasion to the flesh; but by love serve one another."

adults.question: What warning does Paul give about the Galatians biting and devouring one another?
adults.option_a: Take heed that ye be not consumed one of another
adults.option_b: You will be excommunicated
adults.option_c: God will send a famine
adults.option_d: The Romans will punish you
adults.explanation: Galatians 5:15 — "if ye bite and devour one another, take heed that ye be not consumed one of another."

adults.question: What does Paul say about those who are led by the Spirit?
adults.option_a: They are not under the law
adults.option_b: They must still keep all ceremonial laws
adults.option_c: They will never face temptation
adults.option_d: They are guaranteed earthly prosperity
adults.explanation: Galatians 5:18 — "if ye be led of the Spirit, ye are not under the law."

adults.question: Which of these is NOT listed among the works of the flesh in Galatians 5:19-21?
adults.option_a: Slothfulness
adults.option_b: Adultery
adults.option_c: Witchcraft
adults.option_d: Envyings
adults.explanation: Galatians 5:19-21 lists adultery, fornication, uncleanness, lasciviousness, idolatry, witchcraft, hatred, variance, emulations, wrath, strife, seditions, heresies, envyings, murders, drunkenness, revellings — slothfulness is not in this list.

adults.question: What does Paul say about the law in relation to the fruit of the Spirit?
adults.option_a: "Against such there is no law"
adults.option_b: "The law requires all of these"
adults.option_c: "These replace the need for law"
adults.option_d: "The law produces these fruits"
adults.explanation: Galatians 5:23 — "against such there is no law."

adults.question: What does Paul say those who are Christ's have done?
adults.option_a: Crucified the flesh with the affections and lusts
adults.option_b: Memorized the entire Torah
adults.option_c: Been baptized in the Jordan River
adults.option_d: Made a pilgrimage to Jerusalem
adults.explanation: Galatians 5:24 — "they that are Christ's have crucified the flesh with the affections and lusts."

kids.question: What does Paul say we should not grow tired of doing?
kids.explanation: Galatians 6:9 — "let us not be weary in well doing: for in due season we shall reap, if we faint not."

kids.question: What does Paul say we should help carry for each other?
kids.explanation: Galatians 6:2 — "Bear ye one another's burdens, and so fulfil the law of Christ."

kids.question: What does Paul say matters most — not circumcision but what?
kids.explanation: Galatians 6:15 — "in Christ Jesus neither circumcision availeth any thing, nor uncircumcision, but a new creature."

teens.question: What does Paul say about a person who thinks he is something when he is nothing?
teens.option_a: He deceiveth himself
teens.option_c: He will be humbled by God
teens.explanation: Galatians 6:3 — "if a man think himself to be something, when he is nothing, he deceiveth himself."

teens.question: What principle of sowing and reaping does Paul teach?
teens.option_a: Whatsoever a man soweth, that shall he also reap — flesh reaps corruption, Spirit reaps life everlasting
teens.option_b: Good farmers always get good harvests
teens.option_d: Money planted grows into more money
teens.explanation: Galatians 6:7-8 — "whatsoever a man soweth, that shall he also reap. For he that soweth to his flesh shall of the flesh reap corruption; but he that soweth to the Spirit shall of the Spirit reap life everlasting."

teens.question: What does Paul say those who are taught in the word should do?
teens.option_a: Share all good things with him that teaches
teens.option_c: Only learn from scrolls, not people
teens.explanation: Galatians 6:6 — "Let him that is taught in the word communicate unto him that teacheth in all good things."

teens.question: Why does Paul say certain people want the Galatians to be circumcised?
teens.option_a: So they can boast in the Galatians' flesh and avoid persecution for the cross of Christ
teens.option_c: Because Moses commanded it for all nations
teens.option_d: Because it shows dedication to God
teens.explanation: Galatians 6:12-13 — "they constrain you to be circumcised; only lest they should suffer persecution for the cross of Christ… that they may glory in your flesh."

teens.question: What does Paul say about restoring someone caught in a fault?
teens.option_a: Those who are spiritual should restore such a one in the spirit of meekness, considering themselves lest they also be tempted
teens.option_b: They should be immediately expelled from the church
teens.option_d: Only apostles can restore fallen believers
teens.explanation: Galatians 6:1 — "if a man be overtaken in a fault, ye which are spiritual, restore such an one in the spirit of meekness; considering thyself, lest thou also be tempted."

adults.question: How does Paul reconcile "bear ye one another's burdens" (6:2) with "every man shall bear his own burden" (6:5)?
adults.option_a: The Greek uses different words: "bare" (heavy, crushing loads to share) vs "phortion" (individual responsibility/load); community support and personal accountability coexist
adults.option_b: Paul contradicts himself
adults.option_c: Verse 5 is about physical burdens while verse 2 is spiritual
adults.option_d: Verse 2 applies to leaders and verse 5 to laypeople
adults.explanation: Galatians 6:2 uses "bare" (heavy burdens) suggesting shared loads, while 6:5 uses "phortion" (a soldier's pack) suggesting individual responsibility.

adults.question: What does Paul say a man should do instead of comparing himself with others?
adults.option_a: "Let every man prove his own work, and then shall he have rejoicing in himself alone, and not in another"
adults.option_b: "Compare yourself only to Christ"
adults.option_c: "Seek the approval of the apostles"
adults.option_d: "Measure your faith by your giving"
adults.explanation: Galatians 6:4 — "let every man prove his own work, and then shall he have rejoicing in himself alone, and not in another."

adults.question: What does Paul mean by "God is not mocked"?
adults.option_a: God cannot be deceived — the principle of sowing and reaping is an unbreakable divine law
adults.option_b: God punishes those who laugh at Him
adults.option_c: God does not tolerate jokes in worship
adults.option_d: God will destroy mockers immediately
adults.explanation: Galatians 6:7 — "Be not deceived; God is not mocked: for whatsoever a man soweth, that shall he also reap."

adults.question: What does Paul say about doing good to all men?
adults.option_a: "As we have therefore opportunity, let us do good unto all men, especially unto them who are of the household of faith"
adults.option_b: "Only help fellow believers"
adults.option_c: "Help only those who can repay you"
adults.option_d: "Give equally to everyone without distinction"
adults.explanation: Galatians 6:10 — "as we have therefore opportunity, let us do good unto all men, especially unto them who are of the household of faith."

adults.question: What is notable about how Paul closes this letter?
adults.option_a: He writes the closing in his own hand with "large letters," possibly due to an eye ailment
adults.option_b: He seals it with his apostolic ring
adults.option_c: He has Timothy sign as co-author
adults.option_d: He includes a hymn
adults.explanation: Galatians 6:11 — "Ye see how large a letter I have written unto you with mine own hand."

adults.question: What is the only thing Paul says he will glory (boast) in?
adults.option_a: The cross of our Lord Jesus Christ, by whom the world is crucified unto him and he unto the world
adults.option_b: His apostolic authority
adults.option_c: The number of churches he planted
adults.option_d: His sufferings for the gospel
adults.explanation: Galatians 6:14 — "God forbid that I should glory, save in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ, by whom the world is crucified unto me, and I unto the world."

adults.question: What does Paul call the rule of being a new creature in Christ?
adults.option_a: He says "as many as walk according to this rule, peace be on them, and mercy, and upon the Israel of God"
adults.option_b: "This is the first and greatest commandment"
adults.option_c: "This is the way of righteousness"
adults.option_d: "This is the mark of true discipleship"
adults.explanation: Galatians 6:15-16 — "in Christ Jesus neither circumcision availeth any thing… but a new creature. And as many as walk according to this rule, peace be on them… and upon the Israel of God."

adults.question: What does Paul say about the marks on his body?
adults.option_a: "I bear in my body the marks of the Lord Jesus" — referring to scars from persecution
adults.option_b: He had tattoos of Christian symbols
adults.option_c: He bore the stigmata miraculously
adults.option_d: He had marks from Jewish circumcision
adults.explanation: Galatians 6:17 — "I bear in my body the marks of the Lord Jesus."
